narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the courage it takes to put yourself out there. Your profile includes an undergraduate GPA of 2.60, an LSAT score of 27, rankings in the 2nd decile for your first-year school grades and the 1st decile for your third-year grades, a middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 82.9% chance that you would fail it— a high-risk prediction that led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger forecasted success. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your low third-year decile ranking, which stood out well below the typical student's mid-range performance and signaled potential challenges in sustaining academic momentum. Your first-year decile ranking, also in the lower range compared to averages, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest influence toward failure. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA, though below the dataset average, helped pull the prediction slightly toward passing success. Other elements like your family income and LSAT score had smaller effects tilting toward failure, while your full-time enrollment plan offered a minor boost toward passing. Compared to typical students—who often have higher deciles around the 5th or 6th, LSAT scores near 37, and GPAs over 3.2—your academic rankings were the standout weaknesses that outweighed the rest. This doesn't define your potential, especially as a Black woman navigating systemic barriers in legal education. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking mentorship from successful Black lawyers who can guide targeted improvements. Many have turned similar profiles around—your resilience will carry you far. Keep pushing; law schools value growth stories like yours.
